cards.back: A recursive function is a function that calls itself, either directly or indirectly through another function.

cards.text: Every recursive function must have two parts: a {{c1::base case}} (termination condition) and a {{c2::recursive case}} (function calls itself).

cards.front: What happens if a recursive function doesn't have a proper base case?
cards.back: The function will recurse infinitely (or until the stack overflows), causing a crash.

cards.question: What does this recursive function compute?
cards.explanation: Base case: n <= 1 returns 1. Recursive case: n * factorial(n-1) multiplies n by the factorial of (n-1).

cards.question: What does this recursive function compute?
cards.code:
int fib(int n)
{
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    if (n == 1) return 1;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}
cards.explanation: Base cases: fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1. Recursive case: each number is sum of previous two.

cards.concept: Why naive recursive Fibonacci is inefficient
cards.explanation: The naive recursive Fibonacci implementation has exponential time complexity O(2^n) because it recalculates the same values many times. For example, fib(5) calls fib(3) twice, fib(2) three times, etc.

cards.text: Recursive functions use more {{c1::memory}} (stack space) and are generally {{c2::slower}} than iterative solutions due to function call overhead.

cards.front: When is recursion a good choice?
cards.back: When the problem naturally divides into similar subproblems (tree traversal, divide-and-conquer), or when iterative solution would be much more complex. Not for simple loops.

cards.question: What's wrong with this recursive function?
cards.answer: Missing base case! This will recurse infinitely and cause a stack overflow.
cards.explanation: Should have: if (n <= 0) return 0; as the base case before the recursive call.

cards.text: {{c1::Memoization}} is a technique that caches previously computed results to avoid redundant recursive calls, improving performance from {{c2::exponential}} to linear time.

cards.front: What happens to the stack when a recursive function calls itself?
cards.back: Each recursive call creates a new stack frame containing local variables and parameters. These frames accumulate until the base case is reached, then unwind as each call returns.

cards.concept: Guidelines for writing good recursive functions
cards.explanation:
Best practices for recursion:
  • Always define a clear base case first
  • Ensure each recursive call moves toward the base case
  • Avoid redundant calculations (use memoization if needed)
  • Consider iterative alternatives for simple loops
  • Be mindful of stack depth for deep recursion
examples: Good: Tree traversal, quicksort, merge sort
Bad: Simple counting loops, Fibonacci without memoization

cards.text: {{c1::Direct}} recursion is when a function calls itself. {{c2::Indirect}} recursion is when function A calls function B, which calls function A.
